old_claim.note: Military Field Medicine provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Maritime Law Codes provides the shipping-law and port-regulation context for bills of health, ship detention, and port quarantine.

source_shape.source_locator: CDC Emerging Infectious Diseases review describes maritime bills of health, port surveillance networks, ship-captain declarations about crew and cargo origins, and quarantine detention for ships arriving from plague-affected areas.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The review documents ship-focused quarantine practices that used port declarations, health documents, surveillance networks, and detention of vessels from infected areas.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This source pins the dependency to maritime legal and port-regulation practice because the operational quarantine mechanism is ship admission control, not battlefield medicine.

ontology_before: Quarantine public health measures depended directly on military field medicine, mixing battlefield care with port quarantine, bills of health, and ship-detention rules.
ontology_after: Quarantine public health measures depend on maritime_law_codes for the shipping-law and port-regulation context of bills of health and vessel detention.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: quarantine_public_health_measures no longer depends on military_field_medicine and now depends on maritime_law_codes as an enabling port-law prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The quarantine node were rescoped to battlefield disease control or campaign medicine rather than port quarantine and civic isolation measures.
A source showed Dubrovnik/Ragusa quarantine practice depended specifically on military field-medicine institutions.
The maritime_law_codes node were narrowed away from ship, cargo, port, and maritime admission rules.
short_reason: The source-backed quarantine mechanism is port and shipping regulation, not military medical care.
